context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi! I’m working on Problem Set 6 for 6.1200, and I’m kinda stuck on the graph coloring problems, especially Problem 1 and maybe Problem 2(c). I think I sort of get chromatic number, but I’m not confident about how to turn that into a proof.
exchanges.tutor:
Welcome! Let's start with Problem 1(a). The claim says every graph with n nodes and at most 3n−6 edges can be 6-colored, and you need to show it's false.

Before thinking about specific graphs: what would a counterexample need to demonstrate? What two things would your example graph have to satisfy simultaneously?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has introduced themselves and indicated they're stuck on Problems 1 and 2(c). Rather than launching into scaffolding for both at once, I should focus on one problem at a time, starting with Problem 1(a) since it comes first. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question to gauge their current understanding and get them thinking about what a counterexample to the false claim would need to look like, without giving any hints about specific graphs.

exchanges.student: I think it needs to satisfy the edge bound, so have at most \(3n-6\) edges, but also still need more than 6 colors. So I guess I need a graph with chromatic number at least 7?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. So now the question is: can you think of a family of graphs where you already know the chromatic number, and where you could check the edge count against 3n−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the two conditions a counterexample needs to satisfy. This is good progress. Now I should push them to think about what kinds of graphs have high chromatic numbers, to guide them toward finding a concrete example. I won't suggest any specific graph, but I'll ask them to think about what they know about graphs with high chromatic number.

exchanges.student: Maybe complete graphs? I know \(K_m\) has chromatic number \(m\), so \(K_7\) would need 7 colors. I’m not sure if its number of edges is small enough though.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! So go ahead and compute it: how many edges does K_7 have, and what is 3(7)−6? What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with complete graphs. I should prompt them to actually compute the number of edges in K_7 and compare it to 3(7)-6, rather than doing it for them. This is a straightforward calculation they can do themselves.

exchanges.student: \(K_7\) has \(\binom{7}{2}=21\) edges, and \(3\cdot 7-6=15\). So that doesn’t work since 21 is bigger than 15.
exchanges.tutor: Right, K_7 has too many edges. So complete graphs get expensive quickly. But think about it differently: do you *need* a graph with chromatic number exactly 7, or could you use something with even higher chromatic number, as long as the edge count works out? Or alternatively, is there a way to build a graph with χ > 6 that isn't a complete graph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K_7 doesn't satisfy the edge bound. Rather than telling them to try a different graph family, I should ask them to think about whether they can modify their approach — either by trying smaller complete graphs or thinking about whether there are other graphs with chromatic number > 6 that might have fewer edges.

exchanges.student: I think anything with \(\chi > 6\) is fine, not necessarily exactly 7. But I don’t know many examples besides complete graphs, so I’m stuck there. Could I use some planar graph fact backwards or is that not the right direction?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a very promising direction. What do you know about planar graphs and the bound 3n−6? Does that bound remind you of any theorem you've seen in the course?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking in an interesting direction about planar graphs. This is actually relevant — the 3n-6 bound is the planar graph edge bound! I should nudge them to recall what they know about planar graphs and their chromatic numbers without giving it away directly.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think planar graphs satisfy \(e \le 3n-6\) for \(n\ge 3\). So maybe I should find a planar graph that can’t be 6-colored? But now that I say it, I think planar graphs are like 4-colorable, so that can’t be it.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — planar graphs are 4-colorable, so they won't work. But notice: the claim just requires *at most* 3n−6 edges, not that the graph is planar. Can you think of a non-planar graph that still happens to have few edges — few enough to satisfy the bound?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a great connection — planar graphs satisfy e ≤ 3n-6, but then correctly recalled that planar graphs are 4-colorable (4-color theorem), so they can't be counterexamples. This is excellent reasoning. Now I should redirect them: since all planar graphs are 4-colorable, we need a non-planar graph. But the counterexample just needs to satisfy the *inequality* e ≤ 3n-6, not be planar. Can they think of a non-planar graph that still has few enough edges?

exchanges.student: I’m not seeing a specific one. Maybe some weird sparse graph with high chromatic number? I vaguely remember there are graphs with high chromatic number and no triangles, but I don’t know if I’m supposed to use that here.
exchanges.tutor: You're close with the intuition. Here's a small nudge: what happens to the chromatic number of a graph if you add isolated vertices (vertices with no edges)? And what happens to the edge bound 3n−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles a bit and seems stuck. Rather than letting them flounder, I should give a small nudge by pointing them to something they already have in the problem set itself — Problem 2 introduces the Grötzsch graph, which has χ=4 and is triangle-free. But χ=4 is not enough for a counterexample. However, I can hint that they think about whether they can build on simple, familiar graphs. Actually, the simplest approach is to think about K_7 having too many edges — but what about taking a graph that is just a union of complete graphs or something simpler? Actually the cleanest counterexample is just K_7 minus some edges, but that's complex. The simplest counterexample is actually a disjoint union or a graph built from smaller pieces. Wait — actually the cleanest approach for a student is to think about K_7 with edges removed to get under 3n-6, but that's messy. A cleaner approach: note that the bound 3n-6 grows linearly, while chromatic number can be made large with sparse graphs. But the simplest concrete counterexample accessible to this student is probably just taking several disjoint copies of K_7... no wait, that doesn't work either since χ of disjoint union is max χ. Actually disjoint copies of K_7: n=7k nodes, edges=21k, bound=3(7k)-6=21k-6, so 21k > 21k-6. Still fails. What about K_8? n=8, edges=28, bound=18. No. What about a graph that is K_7 but we add isolated vertices? If we take K_7 plus one isolated vertex: n=8, edges=21, bound=3(8)-6=18. Still 21>18. Hmm. What if we take K_7 and add many isolated vertices? n=7+k, edges=21, bound=3(7+k)-6=15+3k. We need 21 ≤ 15+3k, so 3k≥6, k≥2. So K_7 plus 2 isolated vertices: n=9, edges=21, bound=3(9)-6=21. 21≤21 ✓, and χ=7>6 ✓. This works! I should guide the student toward this idea — adding isolated vertices doesn't change the chromatic number but increases n, which relaxes the edge bound.

exchanges.student: Oh wait, adding isolated vertices doesn’t change the chromatic number, since they can use any existing color. But it does increase \(n\), so the allowed bound \(3n-6\) gets bigger while the number of edges stays the same. So I could start with \(K_7\) and add enough isolated vertices.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly! Now make it precise: how many isolated vertices do you need to add to K_7 so that the edge count falls within the bound? Show the calculation.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the key insight on their own. Now I should push them to make it concrete and precise — how many isolated vertices do they need to add, and does the resulting graph satisfy both conditions? I should not do this calculation for them.

exchanges.student:
If I start with \(K_7\), then there are still 21 edges. Let the new total number of vertices be \(n\). I need
\[
21 \le 3n-6.
\]
So
\[
27 \le 3n \quad\Rightarrow\quad 9 \le n.
\]
So I need at least 9 vertices total, meaning add 2 isolated vertices to \(K_7\). Then the graph has \(n=9\) and \(21=3\cdot 9-6\) edges, and it still can’t be 6-colored because the \(K_7\) part already needs 7 colors.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid counterexample, well reasoned. Now move on to part (b): the induction proof from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) works for 6-coloring planar graphs, but fails for this stronger claim. Where exactly does it break down when you try to apply it here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a complete and correct counterexample with clear reasoning. This is excellent work. I should affirm this and prompt them to now think about Problem 1(b), where they need to identify where the induction proof from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) breaks down for this stronger claim.

exchanges.student: I think the proof for planar graphs uses that there’s always a vertex of degree at most 5, then you remove it and color the rest by induction. But for this stronger claim, just knowing \(e \le 3n-6\) doesn’t force that structure in a way that survives the induction? I’m a little fuzzy on the exact failure point.
exchanges.tutor: You're identifying the right area. Think specifically about the inductive step: when you remove a vertex from your graph, the new graph has n−1 vertices. For the induction hypothesis to apply, what would the new graph need to satisfy? Does removing a vertex from a graph with e ≤ 3n−6 edges guarantee that the smaller graph also satisfies the bound?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track — they correctly identify that the proof uses a low-degree vertex. I should push them to be more precise: specifically, why does having e ≤ 3n-6 guarantee a low-degree vertex in the base argument, and what happens to that guarantee when you remove a vertex in the induction step? The key failure is that the subgraph obtained by removing a vertex may not satisfy e ≤ 3n-6 anymore (since n decreases but e might not decrease enough). I should ask a targeted question about what happens to the edge bound after vertex removal.
